title: Loading an electric kiln for bisque firing
subtitle: Stacking and loading bone-dry greenware for the first firing
excerpt: Bone-dry greenware is loaded into an electric kiln on shelves supported by kiln posts. Pieces can touch and stack in bisque firing, unlike glaze firing. The kiln is fired slowly to bisque temperature, typically cone 06 to 04, to harden the work without melting any glaze.
sourceNotes: Bisque kiln loading and firing technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A first kiln firing that hardens greenware to bisqueware without applying any glaze. Typical temperatures are cone 06-04 (approximately 1000-1050°C for electric kilns).
Unfired clay work that has dried to a bone-dry state. Greenware is fragile and must be completely dry before it enters the kiln.
A small refractory cone placed in the kiln that bends when its target heat-work (temperature over time) is reached. Provides a direct record of the firing conditions inside the kiln.
A flat refractory slab that supports work inside the kiln. Cordierite kiln shelves withstand repeated thermal cycling. Always coat with kiln wash before use.

content.text: The bisque firing is the first of two firings in standard studio pottery.
Greenware
must be bone-dry before loading. Any residual moisture turns to steam in the kiln and can crack or explode the piece. Unlike glaze firing, pieces may touch each other during
bisque firing
because there is no glaze to melt and stick them together.

attrs.content: Never fire damp or incompletely dry greenware. Trapped moisture turns to steam and can shatter pieces explosively, damaging other work and the kiln. Operate kilns in a dedicated, well-ventilated space away from living areas — electric kilns produce heat, steam, and fumes on the first firing. Never leave a first kiln firing unattended.

content.text: Steps
Check that all work is completely bone-dry before loading.
Set up kiln shelves on posts at evenly spaced heights.
Load the work; pieces may touch and stack in bisque firing.
Place a witness cone pack on the middle shelf where it is visible through the peephole.
Fire at 100°C per hour for the first 2-3 hours, then ramp up to reach the correct cone.
Checking that work is bone-dry

Press a piece of greenware against your cheek or inner wrist: if it feels cold, it contains moisture. Bone-dry work feels room temperature or warm. Pots that have been drying in a heated studio for one week or more are usually safe. Thicker pieces and enclosed forms take longer. When in doubt, place work on the kiln lid with the kiln set to 100°C for two hours before loading to drive off any remaining surface moisture.
Setting up the kiln shelves
Check the
kiln shelves

are coated with kiln wash and free of glaze drips from previous firings. Set three kiln posts of equal height at 120-degree intervals to support the first shelf above the bottom of the kiln. The first shelf should be at least 40 mm above the kiln floor to allow heat circulation.
Loading the work
For bisque firing, stack bowls inside bowls, cups inside cups, and plates on edge in a plate rack if available. Tall pieces at the back of the kiln, shorter pieces in front. Place a
pyrometric cone
pack (cones 07, 06, and 04) on the middle shelf where it will be visible through the peephole during firing. Pack efficiently; a densely loaded bisque firing is more economical than a sparse one.
Firing schedule

For the first 2-3 hours, fire slowly (100°C per hour) to drive off remaining mechanical moisture and chemical water from the clay. After 600°C, the rate can increase to 150°C per hour. The firing temperature is cone 06 (approximately 1000°C). Once the cone bends, switch the kiln off and allow it to cool with the lid closed. Do not open the kiln until it is below 200°C.
Variations
Fire to cone 04 for a harder bisque that absorbs glaze slightly more slowly. This is useful for intricate pieces that are hard to glaze evenly.
Bisque firing burns out paper clay and wax resist. Do this before glaze firing.

items.symptom: Pieces crack or explode in the first phase of firing
items.cause: Work was not fully bone-dry; residual moisture turned to steam
items.fix: Check dryness carefully before loading. Fire the first phase at 50-80°C per hour to drive off moisture gently

items.symptom: Cone does not bend at expected kiln reading
items.cause: The kiln thermocouple was not reading the actual temperature accurately. This is common in older kilns.
items.fix: Trust the cone result over the thermocouple reading. Cone 06 bending is the goal, regardless of the digital display
